completeness_note: Expanded 2026-07-16 to cover the exam-critical formula set: full EVM (variances, indices, EAC/ETC/VAC/TCPI), both three-point estimates, channels, float, EMV, ROI, NPV, payback, BCR, and Little’s Law. Reviewed as production reference content; further additions go through the same review.

formulas.name: Potential communication channels
formulas.interpretation: The number of possible pairwise communication paths grows quadratically as participants increase.

comparison_guidance.greater_than: More potential coordination paths and communication complexity.
comparison_guidance.less_than: Fewer potential paths; not proof of better communication.

memory_tips: Choose two people from n; divide the ordered pairs by two.
common_mistakes: Using n(n+1)/2.
Treating the result as the number of actual messages.

formulas.name: PERT beta expected duration
formulas.interpretation: A weighted duration estimate that gives the most-likely value four times the weight of each extreme.

comparison_guidance.greater_than: A higher result indicates a longer expected duration under the supplied estimates.
comparison_guidance.less_than: A lower result indicates a shorter expected duration, not lower uncertainty by itself.

memory_tips: One optimistic, four most likely, one pessimistic; six total weights.
common_mistakes: Using the triangular average.
Changing units between estimates.

formulas.name: Total float
formulas.interpretation: The time an activity may slip without delaying the modeled project finish date.

comparison_guidance.greater_than: More scheduling flexibility under the current network model.
comparison_guidance.less_than: Less flexibility; zero commonly identifies a critical-path activity.

memory_tips: Late minus early, using either starts or finishes.
common_mistakes: Confusing total float with free float.
Assuming the network and critical path never change.

formulas.name: Cost performance index
formulas.interpretation: Cost efficiency: budgeted value earned for each unit of actual cost.
memory_tips: Cost asks what was earned for what was spent.
common_mistakes: Reversing AC and EV.
Interpreting a higher CPI as over budget.

formulas.name: Schedule performance index
formulas.interpretation: Schedule efficiency measured in budgeted-value terms.

comparison_guidance.greater_than: Above 1.0 indicates more value earned than planned by the status date.
comparison_guidance.less_than: Below 1.0 indicates less value earned than planned by the status date.

memory_tips: Schedule compares earned work to planned work.
common_mistakes: Treating SPI as calendar days.
Assuming SPI alone predicts final completion.

formulas.name: Estimate at completion using CPI
formulas.interpretation: A forecast of total cost when current cost efficiency is expected to continue.

comparison_guidance.greater_than: EAC above BAC forecasts an overrun.
comparison_guidance.less_than: EAC below BAC forecasts an underrun.

memory_tips: Scale the original budget by expected cost efficiency.
common_mistakes: Using this variant when past variance is atypical.
Selecting a formula without checking scenario assumptions.

formulas.name: Expected monetary value
formulas.interpretation: Probability-weighted value used to compare uncertain outcomes and roll up decision trees.

comparison_guidance.greater_than: A larger positive EMV favors opportunity value; a larger absolute negative EMV indicates greater expected loss.
comparison_guidance.less_than: A value nearer zero has less probability-weighted monetary effect.

common_mistakes: Using percentages as whole numbers.
Ignoring sign conventions or non-monetary criteria.

formulas.name: Return on investment

variables.meaning: measured return attributable to the investment

formulas.interpretation: Relative net return compared with the investment cost.

comparison_guidance.greater_than: Higher positive ROI is generally more attractive when assumptions and time horizons are comparable.
comparison_guidance.less_than: Lower or negative ROI indicates weaker financial return.

common_mistakes: Using benefit/cost without subtracting cost.
Comparing projects with different timing or risk using ROI alone.

formulas.name: Net present value
formulas.interpretation: Value today of future cash flows after discounting and subtracting the initial investment.

comparison_guidance.greater_than: Higher positive NPV generally creates more financial value under the assumptions.
comparison_guidance.less_than: Negative NPV indicates discounted returns do not recover the investment.

memory_tips: Discount future cash, then subtract the initial investment.
common_mistakes: Ignoring the timing of cash flows.
Choosing the shortest project without comparing NPV.

formulas.name: Little's Law
formulas.interpretation: A stable-flow relationship connecting inventory, completion rate, and time.

comparison_guidance.greater_than: At fixed throughput, more WIP implies longer cycle time.
comparison_guidance.less_than: At fixed throughput, less WIP implies shorter cycle time.

common_mistakes: Applying it to unstable or inconsistently measured systems.
Mixing time units.

formulas.name: Cost variance

variables.meaning: earned value: budgeted cost of work performed
actual cost of the work performed

formulas.interpretation: How far the work performed is over or under its budgeted cost, in currency.

comparison_guidance.greater_than: Positive: work performed cost less than budgeted — under cost.
comparison_guidance.less_than: Negative: work performed cost more than budgeted — over cost.

memory_tips: Both variances start from EV; cost variance subtracts Actual Cost.
common_mistakes: Computing AC - EV and reversing the sign convention.
Reading CV as a schedule signal.

formulas.name: Schedule variance

variables.meaning: earned value: budgeted cost of work performed
planned value: budgeted cost of work scheduled

formulas.interpretation: How far ahead or behind the planned schedule the performed work is, expressed in currency.

comparison_guidance.greater_than: Positive: more work done than scheduled — ahead of plan.
comparison_guidance.less_than: Negative: less work done than scheduled — behind plan.

memory_tips: Both variances start from EV; schedule variance subtracts Planned Value.
common_mistakes: Reading SV as money over/under budget rather than schedule position.
Forgetting SV converges to zero at completion regardless of lateness.

formulas.name: Estimate to complete

variables.meaning: estimate at completion: forecast total cost

formulas.interpretation: The expected cost of the remaining work from this point forward.

comparison_guidance.greater_than: More money still required to finish than the comparison point.
comparison_guidance.less_than: Less remaining spend expected; check the EAC assumption behind it.

memory_tips: To complete = total forecast minus what is already spent.
common_mistakes: Confusing ETC (remaining) with EAC (total).
Using BAC instead of EAC when performance has shifted.

formulas.name: Variance at completion

variables.meaning: budget at completion: original total budget
estimate at completion: forecast total cost

formulas.interpretation: The forecast overrun or underrun at project end against the original budget.

comparison_guidance.greater_than: Positive: forecast finish under the original budget.
comparison_guidance.less_than: Negative: forecast finish over the original budget.

memory_tips: Original budget minus the new forecast — negative means trouble.
common_mistakes: Computing EAC - BAC and misreading the sign.
Treating VAC as current variance rather than a completion forecast.

formulas.name: To-complete performance index
formulas.interpretation: The cost efficiency required on all remaining work to finish exactly on the original budget.

comparison_guidance.greater_than: Above 1.0: remaining work must run more efficiently than planned — the further above achieved CPI, the less credible the budget.
comparison_guidance.less_than: Below 1.0: remaining work can run below planned efficiency and still hit the budget.

common_mistakes: Comparing TCPI to 1.0 instead of to the achieved CPI.
Using EAC in the denominator when the target is BAC.

formulas.name: Three-point estimate (triangular)
formulas.interpretation: A simple average of the three point estimates, weighting each scenario equally.

comparison_guidance.greater_than: Higher than the beta estimate when the pessimistic tail is long.
comparison_guidance.less_than: Lower than the beta estimate when the optimistic tail dominates.

memory_tips: Triangular = plain average; beta weights M four times.
common_mistakes: Mixing up triangular (O+M+P)/3 with beta (O+4M+P)/6 on exam arithmetic.

formulas.name: Payback period
formulas.formula: Payback = time until cumulative inflows equal the investment
formulas.interpretation: How quickly the investment is recovered — a liquidity and exposure signal, blind to time value and to benefits after payback.

comparison_guidance.greater_than: Longer payback: capital exposed longer; riskier when funding is tight.
comparison_guidance.less_than: Shorter payback: faster recovery, but says nothing about total value.

memory_tips: Payback answers "when is my money back," never "how much value."
common_mistakes: Selecting by shortest payback when NPV comparison is available.
Ignoring everything the investment earns after the payback point.

formulas.name: Benefit-cost ratio
formulas.interpretation: Value produced per unit of cost; above 1.0 means benefits exceed costs.

comparison_guidance.greater_than: Above 1.0: benefits exceed costs; higher ratios stretch each invested unit further.
comparison_guidance.less_than: Below 1.0: costs exceed benefits — the investment destroys value as scoped.

common_mistakes: Comparing BCRs across radically different investment sizes without noting absolute value.
Using undiscounted figures when timing differs materially.
